metadata.title: Post-Training Flow Debug Double-Check — 6-Factor Factorial Integration
metadata.priority: P0 — validates post-training pipeline produces correct MLflow artifacts for downstream flows

context.summary:
Verify and complete the Post-Training Flow (Flow 2.5) for the factorial experiment. Post-Training operates model-agnostically on trained checkpoints, applying weight-based and data-dependent post-processing techniques. It sits BETWEEN Model Training (Flow 2) and Analysis (Flow 3).
Key principle: Post-Training Flow only applies techniques that do NOT require end-to-end gradient flow (no differentiable pipeline). Techniques like joint denoising+segmentation or differentiable mesh creation are TRAINING concerns, not post-training. See DiffML (Schlosser et al., 2023) for the research vision of truly end-to-end differentiable ML pipelines — we are NOT there yet.
Post-Training creates NEW MLflow runs in the SAME experiment as training (synthesis Part 2.3, Part 3.1). Each new run is tagged with post_training_method, enabling Biostatistics Flow to treat post-training method as a factorial factor (synthesis Part 1.2 Layer B). Post-training method is a NEW factor that EXPANDS the factorial grid — it does NOT replace original runs.
Critical naming convention: Training run: dynunet__cbdice_cldice__calibFalse__fold0 Post-training: dynunet__cbdice_cldice__calibFalse__fold0__swa Post-training: dynunet__cbdice_cldice__calibFalse__fold0__multi_swa
context.synthesis-reference:
This plan is subordinate to the Pre-Debug Run Synthesis: docs/planning/intermedia-plan-synthesis-pre-debug-run.md
Key synthesis sections governing this plan: - Part 1.2 Layer B: Post-Training Factors (post_training_method, recalibration) - Part 1.3: MVRD defines debug scope: {none, swa} x {none, temperature_scaling} = 4 variants/condition - Part 2.1: Flow execution order (Post-Training is Flow 2.5) - Part 2.3: MLflow experiment names (post-training logs to minivess_training) - Part 2.4: MLflow tag schema (post_training_method, upstream_training_run_id) - Part 3.1: Post-Training Flow detailed operations - Part 7.1: Complete 6-factor table (factors #4 and #5 are post-training) - Part 8 Q1: RESOLVED — same experiment as training (recommendation accepted)
context.full-factorial-context:
The complete 6-factor design (from pre-gcp-master-plan.xml): 4 models × 3 losses × 2 aux_calib × 3 post-training × 2 recalibration × 5 ensemble = 720
The factorial YAML is COMPOSABLE via Hydra config groups (configs/factorial/*.yaml). Users create their own configs. The YAML structure is sectioned by layer:
factors: training: # Layer A (GPU, cloud) model_family: [dynunet, mambavesselnet, sam3_topolora, sam3_hybrid] # 4 loss_name: [cbdice_cldice, dice_ce, dice_ce_cldice] # 3 aux_calibration: [true, false] # 2 post_training: # Layer B (CPU, local) — THIS FLOW method: [none, swa, multi_swa] # 3 recalibration: [none, temperature_scaling] # 2 analysis: # Layer C (CPU/GPU) ensemble_strategy: [none, per_loss_single_best, all_loss_single_best, per_loss_all_best, all_loss_all_best] # 5
ALL factors are auto-derived from whichever factorial YAML was used. Zero hardcoded factor names in infrastructure code.

flow-positioning.upstream: Model Training Flow (Flow 2) — produces checkpoints via Docker+SkyPilot on GPU
flow-positioning.downstream: Analysis Flow (Flow 3) — discovers post-training artifacts, builds ensembles
flow-positioning.compute: CPU-capable — does NOT require GPU (weight averaging, calibration, conformal)
flow-positioning.cost: ZERO — runs entirely on local CPU. Only Layer A (training) costs money on GPU.
flow-positioning.non-differentiable-boundary: Prefect flows are NOT differentiable. Gradients do NOT flow across flow boundaries. Post-Training applies model-agnostic weight-space techniques: - SWA (Stochastic Weight Averaging) — Izmailov et al. (2018) - Multi-SWA — multiple independent SWA models - Model Soups — Wortsman et al. (2022) — weight interpolation - Temperature Scaling — Guo et al. (2017) — post-hoc calibration - Conformal Prediction — Vovk et al. (2005) — distribution-free coverage End-to-end differentiable techniques (DiffML) are FUTURE research.

factorial-factors.expansion:
Post-Training EXPANDS the training grid by multiplying with its own factors:
FULL PRODUCTION: Layer A: 4×3×2 = 24 training cells × 3 folds = 72 training runs Layer B: 72 runs × (3 methods × 2 recalibration) = 72 × 6 = 432 post-training variants (24 cells × 6 variants per cell = 144 conditions, × 3 folds = 432)
DEBUG: Layer A: 4×3×2 = 24 training cells × 1 fold = 24 training runs Layer B: 24 runs × (2 methods × 2 recalibration) = 24 × 4 = 96 post-training variants
Cost: ZERO — all post-training runs on local CPU. ONLY Layer A (72 training runs, ~$65 on GCP L4 spot) costs money.

context.debug-scope:
Debug scope reductions (ONLY these allowed): 1. Epochs: 2 (not 50) 2. Data: half volumes (~23 train / ~12 val) 3. Folds: 1 (fold-0 only) 4. Post-training method levels: 2 (not 3) — {none, swa} instead of {none, swa, multi_swa} 5. Ensemble levels: 4 (not 5) — drop "none" for debug
Post-training in debug: {none, swa} x {none, temperature_scaling} = 4 variants per training condition. 24 training runs × 4 variants = 96 post-training variants. Downstream: 96 variants × 4 ensemble strategies = 384 evaluation conditions (debug total).
context.layered-anova-context:
The ANOVA structure mirrors the layered factorial design:
1. Core 3-way ANOVA: model_family × loss_name × aux_calibration (Layer A factors only) — Answers: "Which training factor matters most?"
2. Extended 5-way ANOVA: + post_training_method × recalibration (Layers A+B) — Answers: "Does post-training improve over raw checkpoints?" — Post-Training factors are THIS FLOW's contribution to the ANOVA.
3. Ensemble analysis: + ensemble_strategy (all 6 factors) — Answers: "What is the optimal ensemble strategy?"
All factors are auto-derived from the factorial YAML that was used. Zero hardcoded factor names in the ANOVA code. All statistical parameters from BiostatisticsConfig (Rule #29).

phase.name: Verify Upstream Artifact Discovery
phase.description: Ensure post_training_flow can discover checkpoints from training runs and extract all factorial metadata (model_family, loss, fold, calib).

tasks.task: Write test: post-training discovers upstream training run by experiment name. Verify find_upstream_safely() returns correct run_id. Verify all factorial tags are present (model_family, loss_function, fold_id, with_aux_calib). Tag schema must match synthesis Part 2.4.
Write test: checkpoint resolution finds best_val_compound_masd_cldice.pth. Test fallback chain: best.ckpt -> last.pth -> epoch_latest.pth.
Write test: multi-fold checkpoint discovery across 3 folds. Verify checkpoint_dir_fold_0/1/2 tags resolve to actual paths.

phase.gate: Upstream discovery tests pass with synthetic mlruns.

phase.name: MLflow Run Naming for Factorial
phase.description: Implement the naming convention that creates distinguishable MLflow runs for each post-training method, enabling factorial Biostatistics analysis. Post-training method is factor #4 in the 6-factor table (synthesis Part 7.1). Recalibration is factor #5 in the 6-factor table.

tasks.task: Write test: SWA post-training creates a NEW MLflow run with suffix __swa. Verify the run_name includes all factorial factors + method. Verify tags include post_training_method="swa". Verify the run is in the SAME experiment (minivess_training).
Write test: Multi-SWA creates N new MLflow runs (one per sub-model). Verify naming: {base}__multi_swa_{i}.

Write test: all upstream tags (model_family, loss_function, fold_id, with_aux_calib) are inherited by post-training runs. Tags match synthesis Part 2.4 schema. Biostatistics can discover them as additional factorial conditions.

Implement: Update post_training_flow.py to create correctly named runs. Use SAME experiment as training (minivess_training) so Analysis Flow finds all variants in one query. Reference: synthesis Part 2.3 + Part 8 Q1.

Write test: post-training factors are read from the factorial YAML (configs/factorial/*.yaml::factors.post_training), not hardcoded. The flow discovers which methods to apply from the composable config.

phase.gate: Post-training runs visible in same MLflow experiment with correct naming.

phase.name: SWA Plugin Verification
phase.description: Verify SWA produces valid averaged weights from training checkpoints. SWA is the primary post_training_method (factor #4) after "none".

tasks.task: Write test: SWA averages 3 fold checkpoints into single model. Verify output checkpoint is valid (loadable, same architecture).

Write test: SWA with BN update step (if batch norm present). DynUNet uses InstanceNorm (no BN update needed). SAM3 uses LayerNorm (no BN update needed). Document: BN update is a NO-OP for current model lineup.

phase.name: Recalibration Plugin Verification
phase.description:
Verify temperature scaling recalibration works on post-hoc predictions. Recalibration is factor #5 in the 6-factor table (synthesis Part 7.1). Note: this is DISTINCT from aux_calibration (factor #3, a training-time factor). aux_calibration is an auxiliary loss during training; recalibration is post-hoc recalibration of softmax outputs.
The recalibration factor is defined in the factorial YAML under factors.post_training.recalibration — NOT hardcoded.

tasks.task: Write test: temperature scaling learns a temperature parameter T. Verify T > 0 (always positive). Verify calibrated softmax has lower ECE than uncalibrated. All calibration thresholds/params from BiostatisticsConfig, not hardcoded.
Write test: calibration creates a new MLflow run tagged __calibrated. Verify calibration parameters logged as artifact. Verify post_training_method="calibrated" tag is set (synthesis Part 2.4).

phase.name: Integration with Analysis Flow
phase.description:
Verify Analysis Flow can discover and use post-training artifacts. Analysis discovers training + post-training from the SAME experiment (minivess_training), then creates evaluation runs in a SEPARATE experiment (minivess_evaluation). Reference: synthesis Part 2.3.
Post-training expands the factorial grid: 24 training runs → 96 post-training variants (debug) 72 training runs → 432 post-training variants (full) Analysis then further expands by ensemble strategies: 96 → 384 evaluations (debug) 432 → 2160 evaluations (full)

tasks.task: Write test: Analysis Flow Step 0 discovers post-training models. Verify discover_post_training_models() finds SWA and calibrated runs in minivess_training (same experiment as training runs).
Write test: post-training checkpoints are loadable by EnsembleBuilder. Verify SWA checkpoint has same architecture as original.

Write test: post-training runs are treated as additional factorial conditions by downstream Biostatistics Flow. The post_training_method tag enables the extended 5-way ANOVA (Layers A+B) to include post-training as factors. The ANOVA is layered: core 3-way (Layer A) → extended 5-way (Layers A+B) → full 6-factor ensemble analysis (all layers).

phase.gate: Analysis Flow successfully discovers and loads post-training checkpoints.

phase.name: Local Debug Run Verification
phase.description:
Run the complete Post-Training Flow locally on DynUNet checkpoints downloaded from cloud MLflow. CPU-only, no GPU required. Cost: ZERO.
Debug scope: 24 training runs × {none, swa} × {none, temperature_scaling} = 96 post-training variants. Downstream (Analysis): 96 × 4 ensemble strategies = 384 evaluation conditions. Total debug factorial: 4×3×2×2×2×4 = 384 conditions.

tasks.task: Create make sync-cloud-artifacts target (Issue #882). Downloads mlruns/ from cloud MLflow to local disk.

Run post_training_flow locally on downloaded DynUNet artifacts. Verify SWA, calibration, and conformal plugins complete. Expected output: 24 training runs × 4 post-training variants = up to 96 new runs. These 96 variants feed into Analysis Flow (Layer C).
Verify new MLflow runs are created with correct factorial naming. Check with: mlflow ui --backend-store-uri ./mlruns Verify all runs in minivess_training experiment (not separate).

Verify post-training factors are read from the debug factorial YAML (configs/factorial/debug.yaml::factors.post_training), NOT hardcoded. A different lab could use configs/factorial/my_lab.yaml with different post-training factor levels.

phase.gate: Post-training completes locally, producing correctly named MLflow artifacts for all 96 variants.

questions-for-user.question: Multi-SWA produces N sub-models. Should these be N separate MLflow runs (one per sub-model) or a single run with an ensemble artifact? Recommendation: N separate runs, each tagged multi_swa_{i}, so Biostatistics can analyze sub-model variance.

Model soups (weight interpolation) — should we sweep interpolation coefficients (alpha=0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0) or only use the optimal alpha from validation? Recommendation: sweep in production, optimal-only in debug (MVRD).

appendix.title: Complete 6-Factor Table (synthesis Part 7.1)
appendix.content:
Post-Training Flow contributes factors #4 and #5:
| # | Factor | Layer | Full Levels | Debug Levels | Compute | Config Section | |---|-------------------------|--------------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|------------------------| | 1 | model_family | A (Training) | 4 | 4 | GPU (GCP) | factors.training | | 2 | loss_name | A (Training) | 3 | 3 | GPU (GCP) | factors.training | | 3 | aux_calibration | A (Training) | 2 | 2 | GPU (GCP) | factors.training | | 4 | post_training_method | B (Post-Training) | 3 | 2 | CPU (local) | factors.post_training | | 5 | recalibration | B (Post-Training) | 2 | 2 | CPU (local) | factors.post_training | | 6 | ensemble_strategy | C (Analysis) | 5 | 4 | CPU (local) | factors.analysis |
Full product: 4 × 3 × 2 × 3 × 2 × 5 = 720 conditions × 3 folds = 2160 evaluations Debug product: 4 × 3 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 4 = 384 conditions × 1 fold = 384 evaluations
Cost: ONLY Layer A runs on GPU (~$65 for 72 runs). Layers B+C run on local CPU (free).
Analytical factors (#7-#13) are researcher degrees of freedom for specification curve (Layer D: Biostatistics). See synthesis Part 7.1 for the full 13-factor table.

appendix.title: Composable Factorial YAML Design
appendix.content:
The factorial YAML is NOT hardcoded — it is COMPOSABLE via Hydra config groups. Users create their own configs/factorial/*.yaml files.
Example configs: configs/factorial/paper_full.yaml — 720 conditions (Nature Protocols) configs/factorial/debug.yaml — 384 conditions (pipeline validation) configs/factorial/my_lab.yaml — custom (user-defined)
Post-Training reads its factor levels from factors.post_training in the YAML: factors: post_training: method: [none, swa, multi_swa] # Factor #4 recalibration: [none, temperature_scaling] # Factor #5
The flow code iterates over these lists — it NEVER hardcodes factor values. A lab that only wants SWA and no recalibration creates: factors: post_training: method: [none, swa] recalibration: [none]

synthesis.relation: authoritative synthesis — this plan is subordinate
